narrative: Dear Alex, I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school only to have the door close can feel like a punch to the gut, and I'm truly sorry you're facing this right now. The admissions team used a predictive tool that looks at patterns from thousands of students who've gone before you, and it estimated a 44% chance you might not pass the bar exam down the line, which tipped the scales against your application. What stood out most was your LSAT score of 33; it's the biggest signal the tool saw pushing toward that risk, as it often flags students who later struggle with the bar's intense demands. Layered on top was your Year 3 decile of 1, right at the bottom of your class, which amplified those concerns by showing a dip in performance as things got tougher. Your Year 1 decile of 2 wasn't far behind in influence, painting a picture of early challenges that didn't fully turn around. Coming from a middle-income family added another nudge toward caution, as the tool links that bracket with extra hurdles in law school success stories. Your undergrad GPA of 2.7 pulled in the same direction, suggesting the academic grind might wear on over time. Even factors like being a white male pursuing full-time studies wove into this tapestry, each subtly reinforcing the tougher outlook based on past patterns. It's not that any one thing doomed you—it's how they all combined to sketch a path with real obstacles ahead. This isn't a judgment on your worth or potential; it's just the cold math of prediction from those who came before. You've got fire in you—maybe retake that LSAT, build up those grades, and show them what you're made of next time. Hang in there; your story isn't over.
